title: EECS16A Lab Log: Signals, Systems, and Information Processing
preview: A reflection on EECS16A labs where I learned how signals can be represented, transformed, compared, and turned into working engineering systems.

detail.body:
In EECS16A, I began seeing signals not just as math expressions, but as structured data that can be measured, transformed, compared, and used to build real systems. The labs moved across several connected ideas: voice recognition, spectrogram-based feature extraction, audio fingerprinting, and acoustic positioning. What made these labs valuable is that they connected linear algebra, signal processing, and geometry in a way that felt practical rather than abstract.

One major lab sequence focused on voice recognition. Audio recordings were first collected and aligned so that comparison across samples was meaningful. Each signal was then represented as a vector, which made it possible to use linear algebra tools. SVD and PCA were used to reduce the dimensionality of the recordings while preserving important structure. After projection into a lower-dimensional feature space, classification was done by comparing a new example to the centroid of each class. Voice recognition in this setting is not mysterious. It is a geometry problem. Similar words form clusters, and the system predicts the class by finding what it is closest to.

A later extension introduced spectrograms and richer feature representations. Instead of treating a signal only as a time-domain waveform, the lab converted audio into a time-frequency representation. This made it possible to observe how energy at different frequencies changes over time. This lab changed how I think about modeling. The representation of the data often matters as much as the classifier. A good representation can make a difficult problem much simpler.

Another lab focused on the ideas behind Shazam-style song recognition. The key tool here was the Discrete Fourier Transform, which expresses a signal in terms of its frequency content. A full-song transform shows what frequencies exist overall, but it does not preserve when those frequencies occur. The lab used spectrograms, which are many short Fourier analyses over time. From the spectrogram, strong local peaks can be identified and converted into compact fingerprints. A system does not need to store full audio data to identify a song. It only needs a robust signature built from the most distinctive time-frequency features.

The acoustic positioning lab was especially interesting because it connected signal processing to physical space. Speakers acted like beacons and a microphone received delayed versions of their signals. Cross-correlation was used to estimate the arrival time of each beacon. Once a delay was found, it could be converted into a distance using distance equals speed times time. With multiple beacons, geometry was used to estimate position. Signals became a tool for sensing. Correlation became a tool for timing. Geometry became a tool for localization.

Across these labs, I developed a few core takeaways. First, once signals are represented as vectors, many tasks become problems in linear algebra. Second, similarity is a recurring idea. Dot products, correlation, nearest-centroid methods, and low-dimensional embeddings are all different ways of asking what resembles what. Third, representation matters deeply. A raw waveform, a PCA projection, a spectrogram, and an audio fingerprint are all different views of the same underlying signal, but each view makes different engineering tasks easier.

These ideas can be applied in projects such as offline voice command systems, audio visualization tools, embedded sound-recognition devices, music identification tools, or indoor positioning systems.

media.caption: Voice recognition pipeline: from raw audio to nearest-centroid classification
Signal representation: waveform to spectrogram to compact features
Audio fingerprinting: how Shazam-style recognition works
Acoustic positioning: cross-correlation, delay estimation, and geometric localization
